subject: ap-psychology
title: Social Psychology — Social Facilitation and Performance (EBQ)

documents.content:
Introduction
In this study, researchers attempted to establish whether the presence of others leads people to perform better on a task than when they are doing the task alone.
Participants
Forty-five undergraduate students, all men, received course credit in an introductory psychology course for their participation. Researchers did not report race/ethnicity data for the participants.
Method
As each participant arrived, he was told the following: “In this experiment, you will be part of a group that will perform a task together. It is important that the members of the group have a uniform appearance. To make you as alike as possible, I’d like you to take off your shoes, put these socks over your own socks, and then put on these shoes. They might be a little large, but we need to have a size that fits everyone. Also, put on this lab coat—it ties in the back—over your own clothes.”
Each participant was then given a pair of large athletic socks, a pair of size 12 athletic shoes, and a large, long lab coat. Each participant was led by the researcher to a large waiting room containing tables, chairs, reading material, and some broken equipment.
Each participant was randomly assigned to one of three conditions: alone, audience, or incidental audience.
1. Alone condition: The participant was left in the waiting room alone to put on the clothing.
2. Audience condition: A confederate of the researcher was already seated in the waiting room as the participant arrived. The confederate sat attentively in the corner and watched the participant put on the clothing.
3. Incidental audience condition: A confederate of the researcher sat in a corner of the waiting room facing away from the participant, repairing a broken piece of equipment while the participant put on the clothing.
For all three conditions, the participant was observed by a hidden research assistant who watched him through a narrow opening in the drapes that covered a one-way mirror. The hidden assistant timed the participant as he completed each of the following activities:
1. Taking off his own shoes
2. Putting on the socks given to him
3. Putting on the shoes given to him
4. Putting on the lab coat given to him and tying it in the back
After the participant completed the four tasks, any confederate present left the room, and the participant was left sitting alone. At the end of 10 minutes, the researcher came back into the room. The researcher explained that the other participants had not shown up and that the researcher had called off the experiment because the researchers needed three people to continue.
When the researcher left the room, the participant was again timed by the hidden research assistant while he completed the following actions:
1. Taking off the lab coat
2. Taking off the shoes given to him
3. Taking off the socks given to him
4. Putting on his own shoes
Researchers categorized taking off and putting on their own shoes as simple and well-learned tasks. They categorized putting on and taking off the socks, shoes, and lab coat as difficult and new tasks.
Results and Discussion
Researchers found that performance on the well-learned tasks was faster in the presence of an audience. Performance on well-learned tasks improved both when participants were directly observed (audience condition) and when the confederate was present in the room but distracted (incidental audience condition). When an audience was present, it took the participants longer to complete the new, more difficult tasks. The findings are summarized in the graph.
Audience Condition and Completion Time of Task
For the simple and well-learned tasks, researchers found a statistically significant difference in the amount of time participants took to complete the tasks alone compared with the amount of time it took with an attentive audience.
For the new and difficult tasks, researchers found a statistically significant difference when comparing the amount of time participants took when alone with the amount of time they took when they were in the audience condition or incidental audience condition.
They found no significant difference between the alone condition and the incidental audience condition for either type of task.
Markus, H. (1978). The effect of mere presence on social facilitation: An unobtrusive test. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 14(4), 389–397.

Introduction
In this study, researchers explored how the presence of others influenced the ability of baboons to complete an operant conditioning task.
Participants
The study was conducted on 11 baboons (7 male and 4 female; mean age = 5.72 years; standard deviation of age = 1.71; age range = 3–9 years).
Method
The baboons had access in their enclosure to computers with touch screens, preprogrammed with operant conditioning tasks. When the baboons touched a screen, it triggered the immediate display of a white cross and either a red circle or a blue square. To obtain a food reward, the baboon had to touch the white cross only when the red circle was present. During the non-conflict trials, the white cross was always presented on the left side of the response screen and the red circle or blue square on the right. Incorrect responses produced a three-second time-out.
Figure 1: The Non-Conflict Trial
Baboons performed the task until they correctly demonstrated the rewarded behavior three times in a row.
Once the baboons were trained, researchers increased the difficulty of the task. In 80% of these trials, the baboons were given the setup they were already familiar with, in which the cross was on the left and the circle or square was on the right. But in 20% of the trials, the baboons were presented with an opposite, or a conflicting, setup in which the cross was on the right and the circle or square was on the left. Researchers measured the baboons’ response times when presented with the conflicting task versus the nonconflicting task.
Figure 2: The Non-Conflict and the Conflict Trial
During these trials, the baboons were randomly assigned to be either alone or in the presence of other baboons performing the same task. For baboons performing the task in the presence of others, they could see other baboons using a computer, but the design of the test chamber prevented any of the baboons from seeing the touch screens of the other baboons. As a result, the baboons could not see the other baboons’ responses to the tests.
Results and Discussion
For the baboons who were in the presence of others during the conflicting trials task, their response time was delayed when compared to baboons who were alone. The researchers found that the delayed reaction time was greatest for male baboons in the presence of older males with a higher social rank.
Huguet, P., Barbet, I., Belletier, C., Monteil, J-M., Fagot, J. (2014). Cognitive control under social influence in baboons. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 143(6). 2067-2073.

Introduction
Vigilance is the ability to maintain attention for prolonged periods while still being on the lookout for relevant signals in the environment. Vigilance performance involves participants responding to infrequent signals over time. In this study, researchers examined the effect of observers on people’s vigilance performance.
Participants
Data were collected from 132 participants (98 women; 34 men). All participants were undergraduate students recruited through the research participation system at a large university in the southeastern United States. All participation was voluntary, and participants received course credit for completing the study. The average age of participants was 18.83 years (standard deviation = 2.23 years; range = 18–39 years). Researchers did not report race/ethnicity data for the participants.
Method
For 24 minutes, participants monitored a computer screen displaying repeated presentations of two numbers. They were instructed to press the space bar when they saw two numbers that differed from one another by zero or +/- 1 when subtracted. For example, participants were to hit the space bar when presented with the numbers 43 (4 - 3 = 1) or 77 (7 - 7 = 0), but not when presented with 73 (7 - 3 = 4) or 39 (3 - 9 = -6).
Participants were randomly assigned to one of four conditions:
1. Evaluative observer condition (29 women; 4 men): The participant completed the task with a research assistant present and seated behind them. The research assistant was instructed to sporadically take notes on a clipboard to create the appearance of actively evaluating the participant.
2. Merely present observer condition (20 women; 13 men): The participant completed the task in the presence of a research assistant who was seated at an adjacent desk, facing the opposite direction. The observer could not directly watch the participant and was engaged in a secondary task, like reading a book.
3. Electronic observer condition (25 women; 8 men): The participant completed the task in the presence of a webcam that was placed on top of the computer screen. A video recorder was also placed on a tripod in front of the participant. The participant was told that the research assistant used these devices to monitor their performance. These cameras were not actually recording while the participant completed the task, but the participant believed they were.
4. No observer present (control) condition (24 women; 9 men): The participant completed the entire task alone in the room. No social presence, electronic or otherwise, was used during this task.
Results and Discussion
Participants who completed the vigilance task alone detected significantly fewer correct number pairs than those who completed the task in the electronic observer condition or the evaluative observer condition. Interestingly, there was no significant difference in correct detections between those in the no observer present condition and those in the merely present observer condition.
The results suggest that when people feel they are being directly evaluated by either an in-person or electronic observer, they perform better on tasks that require vigilance.
Claypoole, V.L., Neigel, A.R., Waldfogle, G.E., & Szalma, J.L. (2019). Evaluative social presence can improve vigilance performance, but vigilance is still hard work and is stressful. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 45(5), 616-627.

parts.prompt: Propose a specific and defensible claim based in psychological science that responds to the question.
parts.scoring_notes: A claim that meets the criteria can be awarded the point regardless of whether the responses in Parts B and C successfully support the claim.

scoring_points.description: Proposes a specific and defensible claim based in psychological science that is relevant to the question about whether the presence of others influences performance.

required_elements: The presence of others makes a difference in whether someone performs well on a specific task or set of tasks.
alternatives.correct_example: The presence of others makes a difference in whether someone performs well on a specific task or set of tasks.

required_elements: When someone else is present during a performance, it is more likely that the participant will perform well.
alternatives.correct_example: When someone else is present during a performance, it is more likely that the participant will perform well.

required_elements: The presence of others will make them do worse at what they are doing.
alternatives.correct_example: The presence of others will make them do worse at what they are doing.

required_elements: People should have an audience for tasks they are good at.
alternatives.correct_example: People should have an audience for tasks they are good at.

wrong_examples: Does the presence of others influence performance? [Restates the question rather than making a claim.]
Using animals in research can tell us a lot about ourselves. [Not a claim about performance and the presence of others.]
Studying the factors that influence performance is important to social psychologists. [Not a defensible claim.]
common_traps: Restating the question instead of proposing a claim
Proposing a claim unrelated to presence of others and performance
scoring_points.official_rubric: Proposes a specific and defensible claim relevant to whether the presence of others influences performance. Restating the question, providing a non-claim observation, or proposing an oversimplified conclusion without a clear stance does not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: The presence of others makes a difference in whether someone performs well on a specific task or set of tasks.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; proposes a specific, defensible claim about the effect of the presence of others on performance.

sample_responses.response: When someone else is present during a performance, it is more likely that the participant will perform well.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; takes a clear positive stance on the effect of others' presence on performance.

sample_responses.response: The presence of others will make them do worse at what they are doing.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; a defensible claim even though it takes the opposing direction — any clear directional claim earns the point.

sample_responses.response: People should have an audience for tasks they are good at.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; a specific recommendation-as-claim grounded in psychological science is acceptable.

sample_responses.response: Does the presence of others influence performance?
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; restates the question rather than proposing a claim.

sample_responses.response: Using animals in research can tell us a lot about ourselves.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; not related to whether the presence of others influences performance.

parts.prompt: Support your claim using at least one piece of specific and relevant evidence from one of the sources.
parts.scoring_notes: Responses that use a correct citation style other than the methods prescribed by the question can earn this point for citing the source. Accurate evidence refers to the accuracy of the general pattern of the evidence cited. Since citing specific numbers is not required to score the point, slight errors in specific values do not prevent earning the point.

scoring_points.description: Uses one piece of correctly cited (using parenthetical or embedded citation), specific, accurate, and relevant evidence from one of the provided sources to support the claim.

required_elements: The results of the bar graph (Source 1) show a longer amount of time for participants to complete a new and difficult task in front of an audience than when they were alone.
alternatives.correct_example: The results of the bar graph (Source 1) show a longer amount of time for participants to complete a new and difficult task in front of an audience than when they were alone.

required_elements: According to Source 3, performance was improved when participants were observed by an evaluator and when they were monitored electronically.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Source 3, performance was improved when participants were observed by an evaluator and when they were monitored electronically.

required_elements: According to Source 2, researchers found that the delayed reaction time was greatest for male baboons in the presence of older males with a higher social rank.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Source 2, researchers found that the delayed reaction time was greatest for male baboons in the presence of older males with a higher social rank.

required_elements: According to Source 1, participants were able to perform a well-learned task quicker than those who were alone.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Source 1, participants were able to perform a well-learned task quicker than those who were alone.

wrong_examples: The presence of others impacts what we do. [No evidence cited.]
The baboon study shows that the presence of other baboons made the baboons do worse on the task that required more cognitive control. [No citation.]
In Source 2, researchers used rewards to change the baboon's behavior. [Evidence not relevant to the claim about presence of others and performance.]
common_traps: Citing evidence without a proper source citation
Using evidence that is not relevant to the claim
Providing a vague or general reference that is not specific enough
scoring_points.official_rubric: Uses one piece of correctly cited, specific, accurate, and relevant evidence from one of the provided sources to support the claim. Evidence without citation, nonspecific evidence, or evidence irrelevant to the claim does not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: The results of the bar graph (Source 1) show a longer amount of time for participants to complete a new and difficult task in front of an audience than when they were alone.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly cited, specific, and accurately describes the Source 1 data relevant to the claim.

sample_responses.response: According to Source 3, performance was improved when participants were observed by an evaluator and when they were monitored electronically.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly cited, specific, and directly relevant to the claim about presence of others affecting performance.

sample_responses.response: According to Source 2, researchers found that the delayed reaction time was greatest for male baboons in the presence of older males with a higher social rank.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly cited and specific evidence relevant to how the presence of others (dominant males) affects performance.

sample_responses.response: The baboon study shows that the presence of other baboons made the baboons do worse on the task that required more cognitive control.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; no citation is provided.

sample_responses.response: In Source 2, researchers used rewards to change the baboon's behavior.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; this evidence is not relevant to the claim about whether the presence of others influences performance.

parts.prompt: Explain how the evidence from Part B supports your claim using a psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding learned in AP Psychology.
parts.scoring_notes: The explanation and application points can be earned even if the response did not earn the point in Part B due to incorrectly cited and/or non-specific evidence. The psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding must be explicitly identified in the AP Psychology Course and Exam Description (2024). A concept that appears in the source materials themselves may not earn the application point.

scoring_points.description: 1 point (Explanation): Accurately explains how the evidence from Part B relates to the claim — describes the connection between the specific evidence cited and the overall claim about presence of others and performance.

required_elements: Explains the relationship between the evidence cited and the claim about presence of others affecting performance.
alternatives.correct_example: The data from Source 1 show that people performed faster on a well-learned task in front of an audience, which supports the claim that the presence of others can improve performance on mastered skills.

wrong_examples: Simply restating the evidence without explaining how it supports the claim.
common_traps: Describing the evidence again without explaining the connection to the claim
Providing an explanation that does not reference the evidence from Part B
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: Correctly interprets the evidence in supporting the claim, but does not apply a psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding. Simply identifying evidence or providing an inaccurate interpretation does not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: The evidence demonstrates that the presence of others is generally helpful for improving performance, but if a task is difficult, performance can suffer.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the explanation point; correctly interprets the evidence and explains the relationship to the claim without requiring a named psychological concept.

sample_responses.response: Since the time needed to complete the new and difficult task increased in the presence of others, this shows that the presence of others has a negative effect on performance.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the explanation point; accurately explains how the evidence (increased time) supports the claim about presence of others affecting performance.

sample_responses.response: The studies show that if you are an expert at something, having an audience helps.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the explanation point; correctly interprets the pattern in the evidence in relation to the claim.

sample_responses.response: Performance on tasks is not impacted by the presence of others.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; incorrectly interprets the evidence — the sources show that presence of others does impact performance.

scoring_points.description: 1 point (Application): Additionally identifies and correctly applies a specific CED-named psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding to explain why the evidence supports the claim. The concept must go beyond what is explicitly described in the source materials.

required_elements: Correctly names and applies a CED psychological concept (e.g., social facilitation, arousal theory, drive theory, Zajonc's research) to explain the connection between the evidence and the claim.
alternatives.correct_example: This supports social facilitation theory, which predicts that the presence of others increases arousal and enhances performance on well-learned tasks — consistent with what Source 1 found for the well-practiced task condition.

wrong_examples: Using a concept that is described in the source materials themselves (e.g., if Source 1 defines social facilitation, citing social facilitation earns no application point).
Naming a concept without explaining how it connects to the evidence and claim.
common_traps: Applying a concept mentioned or defined within the sources — this does NOT earn the application point
Naming the concept but failing to explain how it connects to both the evidence and the claim
scoring_points.official_rubric: 2 points: Correctly interprets the evidence and correctly applies a psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding from the AP Psychology CED that is not explicitly described in the source materials. The concept must be explicitly identified and applied to connect the evidence to the claim.

sample_responses.response: The study showed that participants who completed the task when being observed detected more correct pairs than those who did it alone. This is called social facilitation.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the application point; correctly names social facilitation and applies it to explain why the presence of an observer improved performance.

sample_responses.response: The reason for the delayed reaction times of younger male baboons was probably due to social norms. Because the younger males must be submissive to dominant older males, their awareness of that made their performance worse.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the application point; correctly applies the CED concept of social norms to explain the evidence from Source 2.

sample_responses.response: The study showed that participants were able to perform a well-learned task such as putting on their socks quicker than those who were alone. This is an example of the Yerkes-Dodson Theory, which states that well-learned tasks can be performed well even if under high states of arousal such as being watched.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the application point; correctly names and applies the Yerkes-Dodson Theory as the CED concept.

sample_responses.response: When people feel they are being directly evaluated by either an in-person or electronic observer, they perform better.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the application point; explains the relationship between evidence and claim but does not apply a named CED psychological concept.

parts.prompt: Support your claim using an additional piece of specific and relevant evidence from a different source than the one used in Part B.
parts.scoring_notes: Responses that use a correct citation style other than the methods prescribed by the question can earn this point. The evidence and application points for Part E can be earned even if the response uses the same source in both Parts B and D, provided that the evidence used in Part B and Part D is different.

scoring_points.description: Uses a different piece of correctly cited, specific, accurate, and relevant evidence from a different source than the one used in Part B to support the claim.

required_elements: According to Source 3, performance was improved when participants were observed by an evaluator and when they were monitored electronically.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Source 3, performance was improved when participants were observed by an evaluator and when they were monitored electronically.

required_elements: According to Source 2, researchers found that the delayed reaction time was greatest for male baboons in the presence of older males with a higher social rank.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Source 2, researchers found that the delayed reaction time was greatest for male baboons in the presence of older males with a higher social rank.

required_elements: According to Source 1, participants were able to perform a well-learned task quicker when an audience was present.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Source 1, participants were able to perform a well-learned task quicker when an audience was present.

wrong_examples: The presence of others impacts what we do. [No evidence or citation.]
In Source 2, researchers used rewards to change the baboon's behavior. [Evidence not relevant to the claim.]
Using evidence from the same source already cited in Part B. [Must be a different source.]
common_traps: Using the same source as Part B
Using evidence irrelevant to the claim
scoring_points.official_rubric: Uses a different piece of correctly cited, specific, accurate, and relevant evidence from a different source than the one used in Part B. Evidence from the same source as Part B, or evidence that is not relevant to the claim, does not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: Source 3 shows that when people believe they are being monitored, either by a human or a web cam, they will perform better on a task than when they think they are not being monitored.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly cited from a different source, specific, and directly relevant to the claim.

sample_responses.response: Source 2 shows that when the baboons did the harder task, they did worse in the presence of other baboons.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly cited from a different source, specific evidence that the presence of others impacted performance.

sample_responses.response: When participants were taking off and putting on their shoes, they did better with an audience (Source 1).
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly cited and specific evidence from Source 1 that the presence of others affected performance.

sample_responses.response: In Source 2, researchers found that baboons performed worse on the conflicting trial task when they were in the presence of other baboons.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point if Source 2 was already used in Part B — must be from a different source than Part B.

parts.prompt: Explain how the evidence from Part D supports your claim using a different psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding learned in AP Psychology than the one used in Part C.
parts.scoring_notes: The explanation and application points can be earned even if the response did not earn the point in Part D due to incorrectly cited and/or non-specific evidence. The explanation and application points can be earned if the response uses the same source in both Parts B and D, provided the evidence used is different. The concept used in Part E must be different from the concept used in Part C.

scoring_points.description: 1 point (Explanation): Accurately explains how the evidence from Part D relates to the claim — describes the connection between the specific evidence cited and the overall claim about presence of others and performance.

required_elements: Explains the relationship between the evidence cited in Part D and the claim about presence of others affecting performance.
alternatives.correct_example: The Source 3 data showing performance improved when observed by an evaluator supports the claim that the presence of others changes how people perform.

wrong_examples: Simply restating the evidence without explaining how it supports the claim.
common_traps: Providing the same explanation as Part C
Describing the evidence again without explaining the connection
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: Correctly interprets the evidence from Part D in supporting the claim. Responses that do not accurately interpret the evidence, or that just identify evidence without explaining its connection to the claim, do not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: The research shows that being in the presence of others improves performance if you are doing a task that is well-learned.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the explanation point; correctly interprets the evidence in relation to the claim without needing a named concept.

sample_responses.response: The research shows that if you are doing something with superiors watching, you will do worse.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the explanation point; correctly interprets the evidence (e.g., from baboon study) and connects it to the claim.

sample_responses.response: The research shows that whether you are being watched live or by video, you will do better on an easy task such as subtraction.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the explanation point; correctly interprets the Source 3 evidence and connects it to the claim.

sample_responses.response: The evidence supports my claim.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; does not explain the relationship between the evidence and the claim.

sample_responses.response: The evidence shows baboons perform better if they are being watched.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; incorrectly interprets the evidence — baboons in Source 2 performed worse on difficult tasks when observed.

scoring_points.description: 1 point (Application): Additionally identifies and correctly applies a specific CED-named psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding — different from the one used in Part C — to explain why the evidence supports the claim.

required_elements: Correctly names and applies a different CED psychological concept than the one used in Part C (e.g., evaluation apprehension, mere presence effect, Milgram's obedience research, social comparison theory) to connect the evidence to the claim.
alternatives.correct_example: This supports the concept of evaluation apprehension — awareness of being judged by others increases arousal and motivation, explaining the improved performance when participants were observed by an evaluator (Source 3).

wrong_examples: Using the same concept as Part C.
Using a concept described in the source materials themselves.
Naming a concept without explaining how it connects to the evidence and claim.
common_traps: Reusing the same psychological concept from Part C — must be a different concept
Applying a concept mentioned or defined within the sources
Naming the concept but failing to explain how it connects to both the evidence and the claim
scoring_points.official_rubric: 2 points: Correctly interprets the evidence and applies a psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding from the AP Psychology CED that is different from the one used in Part C. The concept must be explicitly identified, applied to connect the evidence to the claim, and not be the same as the one used in Part C.

sample_responses.response: When doing an easy, well-learned task, like putting on or taking off your own shoes, people might be extrinsically motivated to complete the task better in order to get the approval of others.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the application point; correctly applies extrinsic motivation as a different CED concept to explain why the evidence supports the claim.

sample_responses.response: Since the baboons performed worse in front of an audience, that could be an example of increased distress, which could get in the way of their ability to complete a complex task.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the application point; correctly applies the concept of stress/distress (a CED concept) to explain how the evidence supports the claim.

sample_responses.response: The participants in Source 3 did an easy task, such as subtraction, better when they were being watched directly than when they were alone. The participants were not affected by an imaginary audience where people think that others are watching and judging them.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the application point; correctly applies imaginary audience as a different CED concept (distinct from what was likely used in Part C).

sample_responses.response: The research shows that being in the presence of others improves performance if you are doing a task that is well-learned.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the application point; explains the evidence without naming or applying a specific CED psychological concept.
